perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall, during the term of this Agreement and for a period of five (5) years after the termination of this Agreement, use reasonable care to maintain the confidentiality of Confidential Information and to limit its disclosure to only: (a) those of its directors, officers, employees, agents or professional advisors as have a need to know that Confidential Information in order that the objectives of the Discussions and Negotiations can be achieved; and (b) to any of its affiliates or subsidiaries so long as the affiliate or subsidiary understands and agrees that the Confidential Information is being provided subject to all terms and conditions of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party should, during the term of this Agreement and for a period of approximately five (5) years after the termination of this Agreement, consider using some care to maintain the confidentiality of Confidential Information and to possibly limit its disclosure to only: (a) some of its directors, officers, employees, agents or professional advisors if they appear to have a need to know that Confidential Information in order that the objectives of the Discussions and Negotiations might be achieved; and (b) possibly to any of its affiliates or subsidiaries as long as the affiliate or subsidiary seems to understand and generally agrees that the Confidential Information is being provided subject to all terms and conditions of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original text imposes a clear obligation to maintain confidentiality. The modified text introduces several ambiguities: 'should consider,' 'approximately five years,' 'some care,' 'possibly limit,' 'appear to have,' 'might be achieved,' 'possibly to any,' and 'seems to understand and generally agrees.' These weaken the obligation significantly, making enforcement difficult.
perturbation.contradicted_law: PIPEDA - Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act
perturbation.law_explanation: PIPEDA Principle 4.7 requires organizations to protect personal information with safeguards appropriate to the sensitivity of the information. The modified text's vague language regarding maintaining confidentiality and limiting disclosure contradicts this principle by failing to specify adequate safeguards and weakening the obligation to protect confidential information, which may include personal information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: PIPEDA legislation and related regulations - Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada Skip to main content Skip to "About this site" PIPEDA legislation and related regulations About this legislation The Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (PIPEDA) is the federal privacy law for private-sector organizations. It sets out the ground rules for how businesses must handle personal information in the course of their commercial activity. For more information about the application of PIPEDA, please read our PIPEDA in brief page . Schedule 1 of PIPEDA contains 10 fair information principles . These principles are referred to throughout the Act. Administration Privacy Commissioner of Canada — The Office oversees compliance with PIPEDA. Canadians may complain to the Commissioner about any matter specified in section 11 of PIPEDA . The Commissioner may also personally initiate a complaint. Innovation, Science and Economic Development Canada (ISED) - The Minister of ISED is responsible for PIPEDA. The minister recommends to the Governor in Council the adoption of Orders in Council under the Act and tables Government bills in Parliament to amend the Act. Related acts and legislation Several provincial laws have been deemed substantially similar to the PIPEDA . Under paragraph 26(2)(b) , the Governor in Council can exempt an organization or class of organizations, an activity or a class of activities from PIPEDA if the collection, use or disclosure of personal information occurs within a province that has legislation that has been deemed substantially similar to the PIPEDA. This means that wherever the substantially similar provincial law applies, that law applies instead of PIPEDA. Provincial laws that may apply instead of PIPEDA Canada's anti-spam legislation The Digital Privacy Act and PIPEDA Related topics Overview of privacy legislation in Canada Investigations into businesses PIPEDA interpretation bulletins The Application of PIPEDA to Charitable and Non-Profit Organizations The Application of PIPEDA to Municipalities, Universities, Schools, and Hospitals Leading by Example: Key developments in the first seven years of the Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act Additional help Guidance documents to help you comply with PIPEDA PIPEDA interpretation bulletins Privacy Guide for Businesses Contact the Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada Enforcement information Applying paragraphs 7(3)(d.1) and 7(3)(d.2) of PIPEDA How PIPEDA applies to the creation and use of electronic documents Backgrounder on the Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act Best Practices for dealing with pre-PIPEDA personal information (grandfathering) Read the legislation An Act to support and promote electronic commerce by protecting personal information that is collected, used or disclosed in certain circumstances, by providing for the use of electronic means to communicate or record information or transactions and by amending the Canada Evidence Act , the Statutory Instruments Act and the Statute Revision Act . His Majesty, by and with the advice and consent of the Senate and House of Commons of Canada enacts as follows: The complete version of PIPEDA that received Royal Assent on April 13, 2000 . Regulations and orders under this Act Breach of Security Safeguards Regulations (SOR/2018-64) Electronic Alternatives Regulations for the Purposes of Subsection 254(1) of the Canada Labour Code (SOR/2008-115) Electronic Alternatives Regulations for the Purposes of the Federal Real Property and Federal Immovables Act (SOR/2004-308) Health Information Custodians in the Province of Ontario Exemption Order (SOR/2005-399) Investigative Bodies, Regulations Specifying [Repealed] (SOR/2001-6) Order Binding Certain Agents of Her Majesty for the Purposes of Part 1 of the Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (SOR/2001-8) Organizations in the Province of Alberta Exemption Order (SOR/2004
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces several ambiguities that weaken the obligation to protect confidential information, potentially including personal information. This contradicts PIPEDA, which requires organizations to protect personal information with safeguards appropriate to the sensitivity of the information, as stated in the Law Explanation. While the scraped snippets don't explicitly detail Principle 4.7, the second snippet describes PIPEDA's overall purpose: "It sets out the ground rules for how businesses must handle personal information in the course of their commercial activity." The modifications, using terms like "should consider," "approximately five years," and "possibly limit," fail to establish clear 'ground rules' for handling confidential (potentially personal) information, making the obligation unenforceable and creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: For the purposes of this Agreement, the term " reasonable care " shall mean the same degree of care exercised by the Receiving Party with respect to its own information of the same nature as Confidential Information. Notwithstanding the foregoing, the Receiving Party shall be responsible for the compliance by any of the parties listed in subsections 2(a) and (b) above with the provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: For the purposes of this Agreement, the term " reasonable care " could mean a degree of care that the Receiving Party feels is okay with respect to its own information of a somewhat similar nature as Confidential Information. However, the Receiving Party might not be fully responsible for the compliance by any of the parties listed in subsections 2(a) and (b) above with the provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original text defines 'reasonable care' clearly and holds the Receiving Party responsible for compliance. The modified text introduces ambiguity by using phrases like 'could mean,' 'feels is okay,' 'somewhat similar,' and 'might not be fully responsible,' making the standard of care subjective and weakening the Receiving Party's responsibility.
perturbation.contradicted_law: PIPEDA - Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act
perturbation.law_explanation: PIPEDA Principle 4.1 states that an organization is responsible for personal information in its possession or control and shall designate an individual or individuals who are accountable for the organization's compliance with the following principles. By suggesting the Receiving Party 'might not be fully responsible,' the modified text contradicts the accountability principle of PIPEDA.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity and weakens the Receiving Party's responsibility, which contradicts the principle of accountability under PIPEDA. The original contract clearly defines 'reasonable care' and holds the Receiving Party responsible for compliance. However, the changed text uses terms like 'could mean,' 'feels is okay,' 'somewhat similar,' and 'might not be fully responsible,' which makes the standard of care subjective and weakens the Receiving Party's responsibility. The Law Explanation notes that PIPEDA Principle 4.1 states that an organization is responsible for personal information in its possession or control. By suggesting the Receiving Party 'might not be fully responsible,' the modified text contradicts the accountability principle of PIPEDA. The scraped law snippet 2 states that PIPEDA "sets out the ground rules for how businesses must handle personal information in the course of their commercial activity." By weakening the responsibility, the agreement potentially falls outside the rules. Therefore, the modification results in a clear non-compliance with the law.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall issue written instructions to those parties listed in subsections 2(a) and (b) above requiring them to comply with the Receiving Party's obligations referred to in this Section 2 and to use the Confidential Information only to assist in achieving the objectives of the Discussions and Negotiations. Any failure on the part of any of those parties listed in subsections 2(a) and (b) above to comply with the written instructions will be deemed to be a breach of this Section 2 by the Receiving Party.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party should try to give some instructions to those parties listed in subsections 2(a) and (b) above, perhaps suggesting that they consider complying with the Receiving Party's obligations referred to in this Section 2 and to mostly use the Confidential Information only to assist in reaching the objectives of the Discussions and Negotiations. Any possible failure on the part of any of those parties listed in subsections 2(a) and (b) above to comply with the written instructions might be seen as a concern.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates written instructions and deems failure to comply a breach. The modified text uses softer language like 'should try,' 'some instructions,' 'perhaps suggesting,' 'consider complying,' 'mostly use,' and 'might be seen as a concern,' weakening the obligation to issue and enforce instructions, making accountability unclear.
perturbation.contradicted_law: PIPEDA - Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act
perturbation.law_explanation: PIPEDA Principle 4.1.4 requires organizations to implement policies and practices to give effect to the principles, including implementing procedures to protect personal information. The modified text's weakening of the instruction requirement and enforcement undermines the organization's ability to implement effective policies and practices for protecting confidential information, which may include personal information.

perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly weakens the original obligation, creating an ambiguous legal obligation, which contradicts the principle of accountability. The original contract mandated strict compliance, whereas the changed text replaces mandatory language with permissive suggestions and concerns, undermining the enforcement of the confidential information protection. According to the LLM-provided 'Law Explanation', PIPEDA Principle 4.1.4 requires organizations to implement policies and practices to give effect to the principles, including implementing procedures to protect personal information. The modified text's weakening of the instruction requirement and enforcement undermines the organization's ability to implement effective policies and practices for protecting confidential information. Scraped Law Snippet 2 supports this by noting that PIPEDA 'sets out the ground rules for how businesses must handle personal information in the course of their commercial activity.' The changes introduce ambiguity and reduce the enforceability of measures meant to ensure compliance.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that a protective order or other remedy is not obtained or the Disclosing Party waives compliance with the provisions of this Agreement, the Receiving Party shall furnish only that Confidential Information which the Receiving Party's counsel advises by written opinion is legally required to be disclosed and, to the extent possible under the circumstances, shall exercise commercially reasonable efforts to obtain reliable assurance that confidential treatment will be accorded the Confidential Information that is disclosed.
perturbation.changed_text: If a protective order or other remedy isn't really obtained or the Disclosing Party kind of waives compliance with the provisions of this Agreement, the Receiving Party may furnish only that Confidential Information that the Receiving Party's counsel generally thinks is possibly required to be disclosed and, maybe if possible, shall consider exercising commercially reasonable efforts to hope that confidential treatment will be accorded the Confidential Information that is disclosed.
perturbation.explanation: The original text establishes a clear obligation regarding disclosing confidential information when legally compelled. The modified text uses ambiguous terms like 'isn't really obtained,' 'kind of waives,' 'may furnish,' 'generally thinks,' 'possibly required,' 'maybe if possible,' and 'consider exercising commercially reasonable efforts to hope,' which significantly weakens the obligation to limit disclosure and seek confidential treatment.
perturbation.contradicted_law: PIPEDA - Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act
perturbation.law_explanation: PIPEDA Principle 4.5 states that personal information shall be retained only as long as necessary for the fulfillment of those purposes. By weakening the obligation to carefully control the disclosure of information, particularly when a protective order isn't obtained, this contradicts the principle of limiting retention and disclosure to only what is necessary, and ensuring its confidential treatment.

perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces significant ambiguity and weakens the obligations of the Receiving Party regarding confidential information, which contradicts the principles outlined in PIPEDA, even though the law snippets do not directly state Principle 4.5. The Perturbation Explanation clearly states that the change employs vague terms, undermining the duty to limit disclosure. The Law Explanation suggests this contradicts the principle of limiting retention and disclosure. Though the scraped snippets do not explicitly mention Principle 4.5, they do establish that PIPEDA sets out ground rules for handling personal information, implying a need for clear obligations which the changed text fails to provide due to its ambiguities, thus creating a contradiction.
